title: Ὁδοιπόροι καὶ ἄρκτος
source: Émile Chambry, Ésope: Fables, texte établi et traduit (Les Belles Lettres, Paris, 1927), fable 254 (= Perry 65). Text as printed, including Chambry's editorial punctuation (« », ⟨ ⟩ for editorial supplements). Pronunciation: reconstructed Attic, after W. S. Allen, Vox Graeca. Romanization: standard classical transliteration (ē/ō; th/ph/ch; y for υ, u in diphthongs; h = rough breathing; ēi/āi/ōi for iota subscript; accents omitted).

sentences.t: Δύο φίλοι τὴν αὐτὴν ὁδὸν ἐβάδιζον.
sentences.tr: Dyo philoi tēn autēn hodon ebadizon.
sentences.l: Two friends the same road were-walking.
sentences.i: Two friends were walking the same road.

sentences.t: Ἄρκτου δὲ αὐτοῖς ἐπιφανείσης, ὁ μὲν ἕτερος φθάσας ἀνέβη ἐπί τι δένδρον καὶ ἐνταῦθα ἐκρύπτετο, ὁ δὲ ἕτερος μέλλων περικατάληπτος γίνεσθαι, πεσὼν κατὰ τοῦ ἐδάφους τὸν νεκρὸν προσεποιεῖτο.
sentences.tr: Arktou de autois epiphaneisēs, ho men heteros phthasas anebē epi ti dendron kai entautha ekrypteto, ho de heteros mellōn perikatalēptos ginesthai, pesōn kata tou edaphous ton nekron prosepoieito.
sentences.l: And a-bear to-them having-appeared, the one, having-got-there-first, climbed onto a tree and there hid-himself, but the other, being-about overtaken to-become, having-fallen to the ground, the dead-man played.
sentences.i: When a bear appeared before them, one of the two got to a tree first, climbed it, and hid; the other, on the point of being caught, dropped to the ground and played dead.
sentences.n: φθάσας — 'anticipating, beating (him) to it': one word convicts the first friend. τὸν νεκρὸν προσεποιεῖτο, 'kept playing the corpse' — the same προσποιέομαι as the old lion 'playing sick'; fables run on pretence.

sentences.t: Τῆς δὲ ἄρκτου προσενεγκούσης αὐτῷ τὸ ῥύγχος καὶ περιοσφραινομένης τὰς ἀναπνοὰς συνεῖχε· φασὶ γὰρ νεκροῦ μὴ ἅπτεσθαι τὸ ζῷον.
sentences.tr: Tēs de arktou prosenenkousēs autōi to rhynchos kai periosphrainomenēs tas anapnoas syneiche: phasi gar nekrou mē haptesthai to zōion.
sentences.l: And the bear having-brought-near to-him the muzzle and sniffing-around, the breaths he-held: for they-say a-corpse not to-touch the animal.
sentences.i: The bear brought its muzzle close and sniffed all round him, and he held his breath — for they say the animal will not touch a corpse.
sentences.n: φασὶ γάρ — 'for they say': the narrator citing folk-zoology as his source, with the accusative-infinitive of report. τὰς ἀναπνοὰς συνεῖχε: he 'held together' his breathing — the imperfect stretches the held breath out.

sentences.t: Ὑποχωρησάσης δέ, ὁ ἀπὸ τοῦ δένδρου καταβὰς ἐπυνθάνετο αὐτοῦ τί ἡ ἄρκτος πρὸς τὸ οὖς εἴρηκεν.
sentences.tr: Hypochōrēsasēs de, ho apo tou dendrou katabas epynthaneto autou ti hē arktos pros to ous eirēken.
sentences.l: And she having-withdrawn, the-one from the tree having-come-down inquired of-him what the bear into the ear had-said.
sentences.i: When the bear had gone off, the man in the tree came down and asked his friend what the bear had said in his ear.
sentences.n: The tease sets up the fable's whole point: the question assumes the corpse-trick was a conversation. Note ἐπυνθάνετο αὐτοῦ — inquiring 'of him,' genitive, as with verbs of hearing.

sentences.t: Ὁ δὲ εἶπε· « Τοῦ λοιποῦ τοιούτοις μὴ συνοδοιπορεῖν φίλοις οἳ ἐν κινδύνοις οὐ παραμένουσιν. »
sentences.tr: Ho de eipe: « Tou loipou toioutois mē synodoiporein philois hoi en kindynois ou paramenousin. »
sentences.l: And he said: « For-the future, with-such not to-travel-together friends who in dangers do not stand-by. »
sentences.i: « Her advice, » he said, « was this: in future, do not travel with friends who desert you in danger. »
sentences.n: τοῦ λοιποῦ — genitive of time: 'for the rest (of time).' The bear's 'whisper' arrives as a bare infinitive command (μὴ συνοδοιπορεῖν), advice with no speaker needed — which is exactly the joke.

sentences.t: Ὁ λόγος δηλοῖ ὅτι τοὺς γνησίους τῶν φίλων αἱ συμφοραὶ δοκιμάζουσιν.
sentences.tr: Ho logos dēloi hoti tous gnēsious tōn philōn hai symphorai dokimazousin.
sentences.l: The story shows that the genuine of friends the misfortunes test.
sentences.i: The story shows that misfortune is the test of true friends.
sentences.n: δοκιμάζω is the assayer's word — testing coin or metal for purity; γνήσιος means 'legitimate, true-born.' Friendship, the moral says, is verified the way gold is.
